coredata.dc:title: High S-doped amorphous carbon/carbon quantum dots coupled micro-frame for highly efficient potassium storage
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Wei
Zhang, Qingfeng
Song, Shuai
Cheng, Xueli
Yang, Ying
Wang, Longlu
Ouyang, Xiaoping
Xie, Shuhong
Huang, Jianyu
coredata.dc:description: Anode materials with excellent rate capability, capacity, and cycle life have been a challenge in obtaining cost-effective K-ion batteries (KIBs). Based on the concept of waste recycling, we prepared the S-doped (21.5%) amorphous carbon/carbon quantum dots coupled micro-frame (SCMF) by combining chemical exfoliation and S/Se-assisted carbonization. SCMF exhibited the advantages of integrating amorphous carbon and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The CQDs serve as fast electron channels, while amorphous carbon can accommodate more large-size K-ions and mitigate volume expansion. In KIBs, SCMF maintained a high reversible capacity (414.0 mAh g−1, after 100 cycles at 100 mA g−1), a good rate capability (224.0 mAh g−1, 2000 mA g−1), and excellent capacity retention (208.9 mAh g−1, after 2000 cycles at 1000 mA g−1). The molecular dynamic simulation revealed that CQDs provided fast electron transport channels and that C, O and S atoms had suitable interactions with K, facilitating potassium storage. Moreover, the potassium-ion capacitor (PIC) assembled from SCMF and activated carbon exhibited stable electrochemical performance, proving its potential for application. The research provided valuable insights into the reuse of biomass waste in new secondary batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amorphous carbon
Carbon quantum dots
Waste utilization
Capacity retention
K-ion batteries

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of S-doped amorphous carbon/carbon quantum dots coupled micro-frame (SCMF)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the synthesis of SCMF. (b) Digital photos: large-scale preparation of SCMF. (c) SEM image of CSM. (d) TEM image of SCMF and corresponding SAED pattern. (e) TEM image of CQDs at the edge of SCMF. (f) TEM image and corresponding (g) particle size distribution of CQDs. (h) Interlayer spacing of CQDs. (i) STEM image and corresponding EDS mapping of SCMF.

(a) XRD pattern of SCMF, SCM and CM. (b) Raman spectra and (c) FTIR spectrum. (d) Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms and (e) corresponding pore size distributions for SCMF, SCM, CSM, and CM. (f) XPS survey and (g, h) high-resolution C 1s and S 2p spectra for SCMF.

Electrochemical performance of half cells in KIBs. (a) Galvanostatic charge/discharge curves for the first three cycles of SCMF at 100 mA g−1. (b) Comparison of the cycling performance of SCMF, SCM, and CM at 100 mA g−1. (c) Rate performance of the SCMF, SCM, and CM. (d) In situ Nyquist plots of SCMF during charge/discharge for the first cycle. (e) Long-term cycling performance of SCMF at 1000 mA g−1 over 2000 cycles in KIBs. (f) The capacity retention of the SCMF anodes were compared with that of the previously reported high S-doped carbon-based anodes. (g) The cycling performance of SCMF anodes with different active substance loading at 20 mA g−1.

(a) Bonding enthalpy (in eV) of K+ linking to the constructed CQD fragment. Bond lengths are in Å. (b) Side view of the optimized CQD structure. (c-e) Molecular surface electrostatic potential (ESP) color-filled maps (isovalue = 0.001 e/bohr3) and electrostatic extrema (in eV) of CQD, CQD…K+-1 and CQD…K+-2, respectively. (f) Schematic diagram of K+ storage mechanism in SCMF. (g) GITT discharge curve of SCMF (the top right inset shows the single current ladder used to determine the parameters). (i) The K-ions diffusion coefficient DK + of SCMF, SCM, and CM. (h) The CV plots of SCMF at different scan rates. (j) The fitted linear relationship plots of log(i) vs log(ν). (k) The percentage occupied by the pseudo-capacitance mechanism at a sweep rate of 1.0 mV·s−1. (l) Percentage of diffusion and pseudo-capacitance mechanisms at different scan rates.

(a) Schematic of PIC device assembled using SCMF and AC (SCMF//AC). (b) Charge/discharge curves and (c) rate performance of SCMF//AC at different current densities. (d) CV curves for half-cell and SCMF//AC at 1 mV·s−1. (e) The CV plots of SCMF//AC at different scan rates. (f) Ragone plot of SCMF//AC compared to previously reported PIC devices. (g) Long-term cycling performance of SCMF//AC at 1500 mA g−1 over 3000 cycles (the inset shows an LED lit by a fully charged SCMF//AC).

head.ce:title: High S-doped amorphous carbon/carbon quantum dots coupled micro-frame for highly efficient potassium storage

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Low Dimensional Materials and Application Technology of Ministry of Education, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xiangtan University, Hunan 411105, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Taishan University, Shandong 271000, China

Hunan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Intelligent Sensors and Advanced Sensor Materials, School of Physics and Electronics, Hunan University of Science and Technology, Hunan 411201, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Electronic and Optical Engineering & College of Flexible Electronics, Nanjing University of Posts & Telecommunications, Nanjing 210023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Electronic and Optical Engineering & College of Flexible Electronics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Electronic and Optical Engineering & College of Flexible Electronics, Nanjing University of Posts & Telecommunications, Nanjing 210023, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new synergistic approach was proposed to reuse waste poplar sawdust. Novel high S-doped amorphous carbon/carbon quantum dots coupled micro-frame (SCMF) were prepared by chemical exfoliation and S/Se-assisted carbonization. Amorphous carbon and carbon quantum dots coexisted at the nanoscale. The CQDs acted as fast electron channels, while amorphous carbon can accommodate more large-size K-ions and mitigate volume expansion, resulting in high-speed and long-lasting potassium storage. Theoretical calculations further demonstrated the important role of S-doped carbon quantum dots for K-ions storage.

Anode materials with excellent rate capability, capacity, and cycle life have been a challenge in obtaining cost-effective K-ion batteries (KIBs). Based on the concept of waste recycling, we prepared the S-doped (21.5%) amorphous carbon/carbon quantum dots coupled micro-frame (SCMF) by combining chemical exfoliation and S/Se-assisted carbonization. SCMF exhibited the advantages of integrating amorphous carbon and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The CQDs serve as fast electron channels, while amorphous carbon can accommodate more large-size K-ions and mitigate volume expansion. In KIBs, SCMF maintained a high reversible capacity (414.0 mAh g−1, after 100 cycles at 100 mA g−1), a good rate capability (224.0 mAh g−1, 2000 mA g−1), and excellent capacity retention (208.9 mAh g−1, after 2000 cycles at 1000 mA g−1). The molecular dynamic simulation revealed that CQDs provided fast electron transport channels and that C, O and S atoms had suitable interactions with K, facilitating potassium storage. Moreover, the potassium-ion capacitor (PIC) assembled from SCMF and activated carbon exhibited stable electrochemical performance, proving its potential for application. The research provided valuable insights into the reuse of biomass waste in new secondary batteries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As lithium is scarce and unevenly distributed, it is imperative to develop green alternative energy storage systems [1,2. K-ion batteries (KIBs) are considered one of the most promising options for large-scale energy storage applications because of the abundance of potassium in the earth's crust [3,4]. Although KIBs use the same rocker mechanism as Li-ion batteries, K-ions (1.38 Å) exhibit slow reaction kinetics and poor cycling stability in the electrodes compared to Li-ions with an ionic radius size of 0.74 Å [5,6]. Therefore, designing appropriate electrode materials is essential for the fabrication of advanced KIBs.

The development of anode materials, such as carbon materials [7,8], metal alloys [9], metal oxides/sulfides/phosphides [10–12], and MXene-based materials [13], has received significant attention. Carbonaceous materials are gaining more interest for commercial applications due to their abundance, cost-effectiveness, environment-friendliness, physicochemical stability, and tunable microstructures [7]. Among the carbon allotropes, amorphous and crystallized carbon have been extensively studied as anode materials [14]. Routine graphite has an ordered structure but usually exhibits unsatisfactory capacity and rate capability [14,15]. Notably, the inherent nano-size effect of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as one of the minimum nano-sized crystallized carbon particles, leads to a short ion transfer path through the low-dimensional nanostructure [16–18]. Such characteristics help to increase the diffusion rate of K-ion and reduce the volume change of the material [17]. In contrast, the structure of amorphous carbon is typically flexible. There are various ways to store K-ions, including defects, heteroatom doping sites, nanopores, and surface/interfacial storage [19,20]. Unfortunately, the absence of long-range order in amorphous structures will affect the electronic/ionic conductivity within the electrodes, hence resulting in poor rate capability [21]. Moreover, to obtain carbon materials with fascinating properties, heteroatom doping (e.g., N, S, O) has gained popularity in recent years [22]. Among them, S-doped carbon materials usually have high capacities [23,24]. According to the sequence of doping and carbonization, S-doping can be divided into in-situ doping and post-processing. The in-situ doping method, which involves direct doping during carbonization, is widely used because of its convenience [23]. Therefore, it is foreseen that combining the advantages of amorphous carbon and CQDs and introducing appropriate heteroatoms by rational design will help to improve the performance of KIBs.

Herein, based on the waste recycling concept, we obtained carbon semi-microtubes by structural optimization of waste poplar sawdust. Then, the S-doped amorphous carbon/CQDs coupled micro-frame (SCMF) was further synthesized by S/Se-assisted carbonization. Benefiting from an ideal structure, the SCMF exhibited high S doping, close to 21.5 wt%. In addition, the SCMF can be prepared on a large scale due to its cheap and readily available raw materials. Interestingly, we found that SCMF can integrate the high-rate advantage of CQDs with the long-cycle stability advantage of amorphous carbon. Compared to the precursors, adding heteroatoms and a large specific surface area offered more active sites for K-ion storage [22,25]. Consequently, SCMF maintained a high reversible capacity (414.0 mAh g−1, after 100 cycles at 100 mA g−1), a good rate performance (224.0 mAh g−1, 2000 mA g−1), and excellent capacity retention (208.9 mAh g−1, after 2000 cycles at 1000 mA g−1) in KIBs. By coupling with an activated carbon (AC) cathode, the SCMF anode also exhibited stable performance in the potassium-ion capacitor (PIC). This work demonstrates the superiority of coexistence of amorphous carbon and CQDs, providing a new reference for the design of low-cost biomass waste.

Yuxin Construction Co. Ltd. provided poplar powder (20 mesh) for this work. Hunan Huihong Reagent Co. Ltd. supplied nitric acid. Sublimed S powder, Se powder, sodium metal, and potassium metal were obtained from Sinopharm. Aladdin supplied the FeCl3 and AC. Unless otherwise specified, all chemicals were used unpurified and directly.

Chemical exfoliation was used to synthesize the semi-microtubes. First, 12 g of waste poplar powder was mixed with approximately 100 mL of nitric acid (35 wt%) and agitated for 5 h at 80 °C to produce a suspension of golden yellow color. The solution already obtained was then continuously rinsed with pure water until the pH of the supernatant was approximately close to 6 at the last centrifugation. In the end, the powder of semi-microtubes was produced by freeze-drying and pulverizing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of S-doped amorphous carbon/carbon quantum dots coupled micro-frame (SCMF)
ce:section.ce:para: The SCMF was synthesized by S/Se-assisted carbonization. Initially, the desired amount of semi-microtube powder, sublimed S powder and Se powder (1:1:1 by mass) were added into a mortar and ground for 30 min. The mixed samples were heated to 280 °C in an argon atmosphere with 1 °C min−1. After being kept at 280 °C for 1 h, the calcined powder was carbonized at 700 °C for 2 h to obtain SCMF. It can be prepared on a large scale by scaling up the raw material in equal proportions. S-doped carbon microtube (SCM) were obtained without chemical exfoliation using S/Se-assisted carbonization of poplar powder. Carbon microtube (CM) and carbon semi-microtube (CSM) were obtained by the same carbonization treatment of poplar powder and semi-microtubes without introducing of S and Se powder, respectively.

In order to analyze the morphology of substances, scanning electron microscopy (SEM, TESCAN MIRA3 LMH), transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM F200), and atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker Dimension Edge) techniques were used. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET, Beijing JW-BK122W) method was used to determine the specific surface area. Fourier transfer infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was obtained on Thermo Fisher Scientific iS-50. X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 ADVANCE with Cu-Kα radiation) was used to characterize the structures. On the Renishaw inVia, Raman spectra were collected. XPS was performed on the Shimadzu AXIS SUPRA+.

The standard CR2032 cell was used to evaluate the electrochemical properties of the active substance. The oxygen and water concentration of the environment in which these cells were made was strictly controlled to be less than 0.01 ppm. With a mass ratio of 70:20:10, the SCMF, Super P and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), were evenly disseminated in n-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), and a slurry with the right consistency was created by stirring. The above slurry was coated on Cu foil, followed by drying overnight at 60 °C to prepare electrodes. The active substance loading of the electrodes was in the range of 0.6–1.0 mg·cm−2. The glass fiber from Whatman was used as separators, and sodium/potassium thin sheets were used as the counter electrodes to the half-cell. 1 M potassium bis(fluorosulfonyl)imide (KFSI) was dissolved in ethylene carbonate/diethyl carbonate (EC/DEC) as the electrolyte for KIBs, while 1 M NaClO4 was dissolved in EC/DEC containing 5 v/v% fluoroethyl carbonate (FEC) as the electrolyte for NIBs. At least 5 cycles at small current densities were used as the activation process. The collection of Nyquist plots on the same workstation from 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz was made. The galvanostatic charge/discharge profiles were obtained on the NEWARE CT-4008T batteries testing system. On the CHI1660D electrochemical workstation, cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were carried out.

The slurry was formed by homogeneously mixing AC, Super P, and PVDF in the mass ratio 80:10:10 and dissolving them in the appropriate amount of NMP. The slurry was applied to the copper foil and dried thoroughly. The Cu foil was then etched using a FeCl3 solution to obtain the freestanding AC cathode. The SCMF and AC cathode were activated in the half cell at 100 mA g−1 for several cycles and removed for backup, with voltage parameters set to 0.01 V–4.0 V. The mass ratio of SCMF to AC is approximately 1:3. In all PIC tested, the total mass of SCMF and AC was used as the mass of the active substance. All the electrochemical measurements were performed at room temperature.

The Gaussian 09 software package and Grimme's zero-damping D3 dispersion correction optimized the carbon quantum dot (CQD) structure and the CQD…K+ complex [26–29]. Frequency calculations were performed to verify the local minima without imaginary frequencies, and to acquire the thermodynamic parameters at 298.15 K. Subsequently, the difference map of electron density of CQD…K+ was drawn by Multiwfn 3.8 to reveal the influence of K+ on the electron density of the CQD fragment. The variations in enthalpies were used to determine the bonding energy ΔHbd (CQD…K+) at 298.15 K as shown in the equation that follows [30]:
(1)
Δ
H
bd
( C Q D …
K +
)
= H
( C Q D )
+ H
(
K +
)
- H
( C Q D …
K +
)
The surface electrostatic potential (ESP) isosurfaces and the electrostatic extrema of CQD and CQD…K+ were calculated by Multiwfn 3.8 [31–34].

The fabrication strategy for SCMF from waste poplar sawdust is shown in Fig. 1 a. Typically, poplar sawdust was treated with 35% concentrated nitric acid at 80 °C for 5 h (Fig. S1). Afterward, the sawdust was washed by centrifugation to remove residual acid and obtained semi-microtubes. Sublimed S powder, Se powder, and semi-microtubes were mixed, and pyrolyzed under argon to produce SCMF. Poplar sawdust, semi-microtubes, and a mixture of poplar sawdust, S powder, and Se powder were separately carbonized under the same conditions (refer as carbon microtubes ‘CM’, carbon semi-microtubes ‘CSM’, and S-doped carbon microtubes ‘SCM’, respectively). SCMF can be easily prepared on a large scale because poplar sawdust is readily available and cost-effective (Fig. 1b). SEM images show that the CM maintains the rough morphology of the precursor, appearing as irregular tubular structures (Fig. S2 and Fig. S3). Noteworthy, CSM presents different appearance, showing a longitudinally striped hollow structure toward the center, with a microtubule structure remained in the remaining portion (Fig. 1c). The stresses that accompany the formation of oxygen covalent bonds during delignification in the presence of nitric acid molecules open the longitudinal dimensional part of the poplar sawdust [35–37]. The S/Se and semi-microtubes are more easily accessible because to the loose structure and longitudinal openings [37,38]. SCMF was obtained after S/Se-assisted carbonization. TEM and SAED analysis reveal the inclusion of both amorphous carbon and CQD in the SCMF (Fig. 1d). In the edge part of the amorphous carbon, pronounced lattice stripes belonging to CQDs can be found (Fig. 1e). The CQDs can be separated from SCMF by ultrasonic treatment (Fig. 1f). Particle size distribution statistics were performed on more than 250 CQDs to obtain reliable information on CQD size. The results show that their sizes are mainly concentrated in the range of 4 to 11 nm (Fig. 1g). The lattice spacing of CQD is measured to be 0.227 nm under high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (Fig. 1h). The results of HRTEM images processing by fast Fourier transform further demonstrate the crystallinity of CQDs (Fig. S4). As shown in Fig. S5, the amorphous carbon in SCMF exhibits a larger interlayer spacing in comparison to carbon quantum dots, which may accommodate more large-size K-ions and tolerate volume expansion [15,39,40]. These attributes are expected to contribute to improved capacity retention. According to the results of EDS mapping, a uniform distribution of C, O and S in SCMF can be observed, and the signal of Se is almost negligible (Fig. 1i), indicating that S are effectively integrated into the carbon materials. Further analysis revealed that the content of Se was very low, whereas the content of was determined to be S and O is 21.5 wt% and 2.7 wt%, respectively. (Fig. S6). Besides, we observed the morphology of SCMF by AFM to confirm the CQDs distribution (Fig. S8). The elementals distribution of CQDs is analyzed in high angle annual dark field (HAADF) mode, and a uniform distribution of S in CQDs can be clearly found, which indicates that the massive introduction of CQDs facilitate the doping of S atoms (Fig. S9) [19].

In the XRD patterns of the SCMF, SCM and CM samples, there are two broad diffraction peaks near 23.8° and 43° (Fig. 2 a). The two broad peaks are attributed to the (002) and (101) planes of graphite (PDF#41-1487), respectively, and are characteristic of disordered hard carbon structures [15,16]. The broad peaks of SCMF and SCM located near 43° have weaker peak intensities compared to CM, which is related to the crystallinity. The HRTEM images of CM and SCMF show that the crystalline regions in CM is significantly larger than that in SCMF. This may indicate that the S/Se-assisted carbonization inhibits the formation of crystalline regions, which corresponds to the relatively weaker XRD peak intensity of SCMF (Fig. S10). According to the Bragg equation, the diffraction peaks of both the SCMF and SCM samples are shifted to a higher angle compared to the directly carbonized samples, indicating a smaller interplanar distance. Combined with the HRTEM images of SCMF and CM, it is observed that the carbon quantum dots in SCMF have a smaller interplanar distance (0.227 nm) compared to the crystalline regions in CM (0.241 nm). Moreover, there are almost no sharp S/Se diffraction peaks observed in the XRD patterns of SCMF and SCM. Combined with the results of EDS mapping, it can be inferred that S may enter the carbon matrix through doping. In Raman spectra, the peaks at 1330 cm−1 and 1572 cm−1, respectively, are close to the locations of the D-band for disordered carbon and the G-band for sp2 hybridized carbon (Fig. 2b) [15]. The peak-intensity ratios of the D and G bands (ID/IG) can be used to evaluate carbon materials' defect and disorder levels. A peak at 2800 cm−1 is attributed to 2D and D + D' bands approximately, confirming the weak crystallization structure of SCMF [19]. SCMF and SCM show an additional peak at about 350 cm−1 attributed to CS bond stretching vibrations. CM, SCM, and SCMF have ID/IG values of 0.81, 0.83, and 0.86, respectively. The larger ID/IG ratio in SCMF compared to CM and SCM indicates a greater degree of disorder and more defects, which are associated with the introduction of heteroatoms [22]. Nevertheless, graphitization is also present in SCMF. Through the use of FT-IR, the functional groups are examined and verified (Fig. 2c). The stretching vibration of CS, CO, SO, and CS, respectively, correspond to the absorption peaks at around 1168, 1010, 845, and 664 cm−1, which demonstrate the success of the introduction of S and O atoms.

The specific surface area and pore information of CM, CSM, SCM, and SCMF are analyzed using a nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm (Fig. 2d, e). The specific surface areas of the materials are measured as 8.6, 488.6, 655.3, and 845.1 m2 g−1 for CM, CSM, SCM, and SCMF, respectively. The synergistic combination of chemical exfoliation and S/Se-assisted carbonization increases the specific surface area of SCMF in comparison to acid treatment alone and S/Se-assisted carbonation alone. CM displays a nitrogen absorption/desorption isotherm of type II. This suggests that the substance has a solid structure and just a few pores, which are mostly concentrated at 2.6 nm. Compared to CM, the nitrogen absorption/desorption isotherm of CSM, SCM, and SCMF show type I(b), indicating the presence of abundant microporous in the material, with the pore sizes being concentrated at 1.941, 1.944, and 1.960 nm, respectively. A large number of micropores provide more leaching channels for electrolyte ions, thus facilitating the interaction between the electrode and electrolyte. These micropores also provide additional active sites for the efficient adsorption of K-ions [25,41]. To investigate the role played by Se in the material, the poplar powder underwent the same chemical exfoliation process. Subsequently, carbon micro-frame-1 (CMF-1) and carbon micro-frame-2 (CMF-2) were prepared through Se-assisted carbonization and S-assisted carbonization, respectively. As shown in Fig. S11, the specific surface areas of CMF-1 and CMF-2 were measured as 550.7 and 517.3 m2 g−1. These results indicate that the addition of Se is beneficial to increase the specific surface area of the material. The large specific surface area may favor potassium storage and improve the adsorption behaviour [42–44]. In addition, the thermodynamic equilibrium calculations related to Se show that Se oxides are the main gaseous Se species during combustion, while the dominant gaseous Se species are H2Se (g) and Se (g) under gasification/pyrolysis condition. Under high-temperature pyrolysis conditions, the Se powder is converted to gaseous Se, which is subsequently carried away by the tube furnace gas stream [45]. Nevertheless, a small amount of Se doping may still be present. To reveal the S/O doping state in SCMF, we have characterized it using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The peaks at about 532, 285, 228 and 164 eV correspond to O 1s, C 1s, S 2s and S 2p peaks, respectively. Compared with SCM and SCMF, CM does not exhibit any S 2s and S 2p peaks. (Fig. 2f). Fig. 2g depicts the decomposition of the high-resolution C 1s spectrum into three peaks: CC (approximately 284.8 eV), CS (approximately 286.3 eV), and CO (approximately 288.4 eV). Moreover, the high-resolution O 1s spectrum of SCMF can also be matched to three peaks (Fig. S12): CO (approximately 532.1 eV), CO (approximately 533.2 eV), and COOR (approximately 535.8 eV). The four peaks in the S 2p spectrum can be classified into three types, attributed to CS bonds (164.0 and 165.3 eV), SC bond (162.0 eV), and SO bond (167.8 eV), respectively (Fig. 2h). The presence of SC bonds indicates that sulfur atoms have successfully been incorporated into the interior of SCMF through a doping process.

Cyclic voltammetry and galvanostatic charge/discharge cycles are used in KIBs to determine the electrochemical characteristics of SCMF. Fig. S13 shows a series of CV curves for the SCMF in the range of 0.01–3 V with a scan rate of 0.1 mV·s−1. A distinct peak in the region of 1.0–0.5 V can be observed in the initial cathodic scan. This peak gradually disappears in the subsequent cycles, which may be attributed to the formation of local solid electrolyte interface (SEI) films and side reactions. The CV curves also display two redox peaks at 1.85/0.67 V approximately, corresponding to the S and K+ redox processes. Moreover, the similarity between second and third CV curves of SCMF implies its favorable reversibility performance. Fig. 3 a and Fig. S14a, b show the charge/discharge curves of SCMF, SCM and CM during their initial three cycles, respectively. The voltage plateaus of SCMF in the charge/discharge process correspond to the redox peaks in the CV curves. The slop of curves indicates the adsorption of K-ions onto defective and heteroatom-rich carbon surfaces [46]. Fig. 3b shows the cycling performance of SCMF, SCM, and CM at 100 mA g−1. Compared to SCM (280.8 mAh g−1, after 100 cycles) and CM (176.3 mAh g−1), SCMF shows a higher reversible capacity (414.0 mAh g−1). Notably, SCMF can maintain an average Coulomb efficiency of more than 98% during the cycling process after the stable formation of SEI. Fig. 3c shows the rate capability of SCMF, SCM and CM at different current densities, respectively. The SCMF demonstrates a maximum reversible discharging capacity of 400.0, 370.3, 330.0, 303.3, 286.2, 272.2, and 224.0 mAh g−1 at 100, 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000, and 2000 mA g−1, respectively. Meanwhile, when the current density is returned to 100 mA g−1, the reversible specific capacity of SCMF regains 410.7 mAh g−1, displaying a favorable rate performance. Understanding the process of SEI film formation is essential for improving electrochemical performance, which was explored in detail by in situ electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. In the high-frequency region of the Nyquist diagram, the charge transfer resistance (Rct) is shown as a semicircle [16]. The change in resistance during charge/discharge process is analyzed by means of a Nyquist diagram, Prior to that, the corresponding equivalent circuit is simulated to assist the analysis (Fig. S15). As depicted in Fig. 3d, the Rct of the open circuit voltage (OCV) is roughly three times 1.6 V during the initial discharge of the SCMF. As K+ gradually embeds in the SCMF, Rct significantly reduces, indicating the formation of conductive SEI films at this stage. The Rct values remained stable between 1.6 and 0.1 V when discharged to 0.01 V, indicating the formation of metastable SEI film at high potentials. During the initial charging process, the Rct value decreases from 3714.1 Ω to approximately 921.5 Ω as the voltage value increases from 0.01 V to 2.6 V. This indicates that the depotassiation process possesses a faster charge transfer. Besides, a modest current density of 1000 mA g−1 is used to assess the long-term cycling performance of SCMF and CM (Fig. 3e and Fig. S16). Benefiting from the flexible structure of amorphous carbon, the reversible discharge specific capacity of SCMF remains 208.9 mAh g−1 after 2000 cycles with a capacity retention of 84.9%, showing a superior cycle lifespan. As shown in Fig. 3f, the capacity retention of SCMF under long-term cycling is better than most of the high S-doped carbon-based anodes reported so far [24,47–52]. Fig. 3g and Fig. S17 show that SCMF still provides stable high specific capacity of 401, 382.3, 359.6, 346.5, and 307 mAh g−1 after 30 cycles at loads of 1.16, 2.22, 2.59, 2.79, and 5.02 mg·cm−2. These results indicate that SCMF may have a good fast electron transport capacity and application potential.

Furthermore, we also discuss the Na-storage properties of SCMF, SCM and CM in NIBs. The CV curve of SCMF shows two redox peaks at about 1.82/1.07 V, attributed to S and Na+ redox processes (Fig. S18a). The CV curves nearly overlap after the initial capacity loss, indicating that the SCMF anode in NIBs also has good capacity retention. During the first three cycles of the charge/discharge process (Fig. S18b), the voltage plateau position corresponds well with the redox peak in the CV curve. After 100 cycles at 100 mA g−1, SCMF, SCM, and CM exhibit a discharge specific capacity of 416.2, 327.4, and 143.2 mAh g−1, respectively (Fig. S18c). Furthermore, the SCMF is approximately 423.2, 343.3, 311.7, 295.0, 281.0, 276.6, 233.9, and 203.4 mAh g−1 at 100, 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000, 2000, and 4000 mA g−1, respectively (Fig. S18d). Notably, it can maintain a high discharge specific capacity of ∼203.4 mAh g−1 even at 4000 mA g−1. When the current density returns to 100 mA g−1, the specific capacity can still reach ∼406.1 mAh g−1, indicating that SCMF has excellent reversibility in NIBs. The long-term Na-storage lifespan of SCMF is evaluated at 1000 mA g−1. After 1000 cycles, the reversible capacity remains at about 282.5 mAh g−1 with almost no capacity decay (Fig. S18e), much higher than that of CM (Fig. S20). The excellent Na/K-ion storage capacity proves the superiority of coexistence of amorphous carbon and CQDs. The rate performance of the half-cell is enhanced by the CQDs distributing between the amorphous carbon, which offers a significant number of quick electron transport channels and additional active sites [19]. In addition, due to the flexible structure of amorphous carbon and high S content doping, SCMF can maintain a long cycle life and high discharge specific capacity.

The role played by CQD in the potassium storage process is investigated based on density functional theory (DFT). An S-doped CQD was constructed with an S atom to replace a graphene C atom. The optimized cartesian coordinates of all optimized structures in the present work are listed in Table. S1, and their optimized structures and bonding energy ΔHbd (CQD…K+) value are shown in Fig. 4 a. No matter from the opposite side (CQD…K+-1) or the same side (CQD…K+-2) against the S atom, K+ adheres to a benzene ring neighboring to the S atom, suggesting that K+ is still deposited in graphene layers. Compared with previous computational results about potassium storage by graphene, the bonding enthalpies of K+ with CQD…K+-1 (−0.75 eV) and CQD…K+-2 (−0.66 eV) are much lower than that of K+ with graphene, which confirms that the interactions between K+ and CQDs are strengthened. In CQD…K+-2, the S atom repels K+, and pushes K+ to deviate from the centroid of the benzene ring. The side view of the optimized CQD frame shows that the interior S atom will wrinkle the graphene fragments into smaller microspheres (Fig. 4b). Fig. 4c demonstrates that the S atom in CQD is positively charged. The molecular electrostatic potential (ESP) isosurfaces of CQD…K+-1 and CQD…K+-2 in Fig. 4d and Fig. 4e reveal that K+ makes the local region of CQD positively charged.

We also established a CQD fragment with the marginal S atoms to be oxidized to SO groups, as depicted in Fig. S21, in order to discuss the influence of SO groups on potassium storage. It is interesting that K+ will be attached to the peripheral O and C atoms rather than to a bridged S atom. The difference map of electron density of CQD…K+ in Fig. S21b illustrates that K+ can obviously seize electrons from CQD, and the electron density of the CQD region is apparently decreased. Fig. S21b exhibits the strong electrostatic interactions of K+ with the O, C and S atoms. Fig. S21c, d show that, the electrostatic minima (−1.94 and −1.52 eV) locate at these two preferred O and C atoms, which strongly attract the K-ion to generate the most stable complex. The estimated bonding enthalpy is 1.66 eV, which is much higher than those of the graphene and graphene oxides [53]. In conclusion, the molecular dynamic simulation revealed that CQDs provided fast electron transport channels and that C, O and S atoms had suitable interactions with K, facilitating potassium storage [22]. Based on the results already obtained, a schematic diagram of potassium storage in SCMF can be summarized (Fig. 4f). During the process of potassiation, both CQDs and S atoms in the amorphous carbon have a positive adsorption effect on K+. A small number of irreversible chemical reactions produce compounds of K and S that will eventually become part of the SEI membrane [47].

To further investigate the kinetics of K-ion diffusion SCMF, SCM, and CM during the discharge process, the galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) is used. Fig. 4g shows the GITT curve of SCMF with a series of pulsed currents applied at 100 mA g−1 for 20 min followed by 40 min of relaxation. Based on the results already derived, the following formula (2) can be used to determine the K-ions diffusion coefficient DK + [54].
(2)
D
K +
=
4
π τ
m B
V M
M B
S
2
Δ
E S
Δ
E τ
2
where τ,
m B
and
M B
are the duration of the current pulse (s), active material mass, and molar mass of SCMF, respectively.
V M
is the molar volume of the electrode material.
S
is the actual area of the electrode. ΔEs and ΔEτ represent the amount of voltage change in the constant current phase. As shown in Fig. S22, the linear relationship between V and τ1/2 simplifies equation (2) to equation (3) [55].
(3)
D
K +
=
4
L 2
π τ

L
is the electrode's thickness when applied to a half cell. Fig. 4h shows the diffusion coefficient of K-ions (DK +) calculation for the three samples. It is evident that SCMF exhibits a higher DK+ compared to CM and SCM, which explains its superior electrochemical performance. Compared to SCM and CM, the larger specific surface area of SCMF provides an adequate electrode-electrolyte interface, while the numerous micropores of the material shorten the diffusion path of K ions and thus facilitates ion transfer [15].

We also analyze the relevant charge storage mechanism. As shown in Fig. 4i, CV curves are tested to evaluate the percentage of pseudo-capacitance control and diffusion control of SCMF at different sweep rates. When the scan rate is increased from 0.1 to 1.0 mV·s−1, the CV shape did not change significantly, exhibiting good transfer kinetics. Peak current (i) and sweep rate (v) are maintained as a power law relationship (4) [8].
(4)
i = α
v b

The slope value from the linear fit of log(i) to log(v) can be used to calculate the value of b in the equation. The magnitude of the b value can be used to initially determine the charge storage mechanism, with b = 0.5 representing full dominance by diffusion control and b = 1 representing full dominance by pseudo-capacitance control [19]. The b values of the cathode/anode peaks of SCMF calculated from the fitted curves are 0.93 and 0.66, respectively, showing that the potassium storage process of SCMF is controlled by both diffusion and pseudo-capacitance (Fig. 4j). Where the contribution of the pseudo-capacitance control at different sweep speeds is calculated from equation (5) [14,16]:
(5)
i=
k 1
v+
k 2
v
1/2

The
k 1
v
and
k 2
v
1 / 2
parts of the equation represent the contributions of the pseudo-capacitance and diffusion control, respectively. According to Fig. 4k, the contribution of the pseudo-capacitance control in charge storage is calculated to be 50.20% at a scan rate of 1.0 mV s−1. The percentage of the pseudo-capacitance control at various sweep speeds is further shown in Fig. 4l. At lower scan rates (below 1.0 mV·s−1), the diffusion control dominates, where the charge storage capacity comes from the embedding of K-ion. Although CQDs are distributed in the amorphous carbon matrix, the small size of CQDs hardly hinders the deintercalation of K ions at low rates [17]. As the scan rate increases, the percentage of surface adsorption-dominated pseudo-capacitance control gradually increases until it dominates. These results suggest that pseudo-capacitance is essential in storing K-ions at high-rate conditions, leading to excellent rate performance and capacity retention [55].

To verify the superiority of SCMF in practical applications, we assembled PIC devices (SCMF//AC) by pairing the SCMF anode with the AC cathode (Fig. 5 a). Fig. 5b shows the charge/discharge curves of SCMF//AC at different current densities as the voltage is in the range of from 0.01–4 V. When the energy density is 100 W·h·kg−1 and the power density is 782 W·kg−1, the SCMF//AC can run for up to 1041 s. Besides, the energy density of SCMF//AC are 100, 71, 42, and 17 W·h·kg−1 at power densities of 782, 1425, 2447, and 3756 W·kg−1, respectively (Fig. 5c). The CV curves of SCMF, AC, and SCMF//AC at 1 mV·s−1 are shown in Fig. 5d. The CV plots of SCMF//AC at different scan rates display predominantly rectangular shape without obvious redox peaks appearing, which indicates that the capacitive behavior dominates the electrochemical process of the device (Fig. 5e). SCMF//AC exhibits high energy storage compared to previously reported PIC devices such as NCNTS//AC [56], SC//AC [57], NGC//KTO [58], PB//AC [59], Graphite//AC [60], and AC//AC devices (Fig. 5f) [61]. It is noteworthy that SCMF//AC at 1500 mA g−1 for more than 3000 cycles did not cause a decay in energy density (Fig. 5g). The fully charged SCMF//AC can light up a red LED, which shows its excellent application potential (inset in Fig. 5g).

In conclusion, based on the concept of waste recycling, this work reported a simple and novel strategy (Chemical stripping and S/Se-assisted carbonization synergistic treatments) for the large-scale synthesis of SCMF from waste poplar sawdust for K-ion storage. The unique amorphous carbon/CQDs coexistence framework combined the structure and properties of both amorphous carbon and CQDs. The structural advantage brought high S-doping (21.5 wt%) to SCMF. The CQDs provided fast electron transport channels and additional ion-storage active sites. The molecular dynamic simulation revealed that C, O and S atoms exhibited favorable interactions with K, thereby facilitating potassium storage. In KIBs, SCMF maintained a high reversible capacity (414.0 mAh g−1, after 100 cycles at 100 mA g−1), a good rate performance (224.0 mAh g−1, 2000 mA g−1), and excellent capacity retention (208.9 mAh g−1, after 2000 cycles at 1000 mA g−1). The capacity retention of SCMF (84.9%) under long-term cycling was better than most of the high S-doped carbon-based anodes reported so far (Table. S2). Furthermore, PIC devices (SCMF//AC) assembled by SCMF and AC had stable electrochemical performance, indicating SCMF had promise for applications.

Wei Xie: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Visualization, Formal analysis, Project administration, Validation, Writing - original draft. Qingfeng Zhang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Shuai Song: Investigation. Xueli Cheng: Investigation, Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Ying Yang: Investigation, Supervision. Longlu Wang: Supervision. Xiaoping Ouyang: Resources, Supervision. Shuhong Xie: Resources, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Jianyu Huang: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.
